MC: Which sequence describes the split-apply-combine pattern used by pandas groupby?

column3: Split the data into groups based on keys, apply a function to each group, and combine the results into a single output
Sort the data by a key, run a loop over each unique value, and append each result to a list
Filter the data down to one group at a time, export each group to a separate file, and merge the files at the end
Index into the DataFrame with a list of keys, transform each column, and concatenate the columns back together

MC: When you call df.groupby('key_column'), what is returned?

column3: A dictionary mapping each unique key to the rows of the original DataFrame
A DataFrameGroupBy object that holds the grouping information but has not yet performed any computation
A DataFrame with one row per unique key value and no other columns
A list of smaller DataFrames, one for each unique group key

MC: Which of the following CANNOT be used as a grouping key passed to groupby()?

column3: The name of a column in the DataFrame
A single integer value, such as 5
A Series or array the same length as the axis being grouped
A function invoked on each value of the axis index

MC: How can you iterate over the groups produced by df.groupby('key'), one at a time?

column3: for name, group in df.groupby('key'): each iteration yields a tuple of (group_name, group_DataFrame)
for group in df.groupby('key'): each iteration yields a DataFrame only
You cannot iterate over a GroupBy object; you must first call .size() or .apply()
for key in df.groupby('key'): each iteration yields the group key, then you index into the DataFrame separately

MC: What is the difference between .size() and .count() on a GroupBy object?

column3: .size() counts only the numeric columns; .count() counts all columns
.size() returns the number of unique values per group; .count() returns the total number of rows per group
.size() is a deprecated alias for .count(); they produce identical output in current pandas
.size() returns the row count per group; .count() returns the count of non-null values per column

MC: By default, what does groupby() do with rows whose grouping key is NaN?

column3: It raises an error, requiring the user to clean the data first
It places those rows in a group labeled 'NaN' that appears in the result
It excludes those rows from the result entirely
It assigns those rows to the first existing group alphabetically

MC: What does passing dropna=False to groupby() change?

column3: NaN values in the aggregated columns (not the group key) are included rather than skipped during aggregation
Missing values are replaced with the column mean before grouping
Rows with NaN in the grouping key are kept and appear as their own NaN group in the result
Missing values in both the grouping key and the aggregated columns are preserved as NaN in the output

MC: When you group by a list of keys, such as df.groupby(['key1', 'key2']), what does the result's index look like after aggregation?

column3: A single-level index whose values are tuples of the grouping key values
A single-level index that concatenates the key values into one string per row
A default integer index with the grouping keys retained as regular columns
A hierarchical index (MultiIndex) with one level per grouping key, where each row corresponds to a unique combination of key values

MC: Which of the following is NOT one of the optimized GroupBy aggregation methods listed in Chapter 10?

column3: mean
quantile
concat
first

MC: What does grouped.agg('mean') produce for a single-column GroupBy?

column3: A DataFrame with the mean of every column in the original DataFrame
The same result as grouped.mean(): a Series with one value per group
A scalar: the mean of all values across all groups
A DataFrame with one column called 'mean' and one row per group
A Series with three values: the overall mean, std, and count
A list of three Series, one for each function
A DataFrame with the three function names as rows and the groups as columns
A DataFrame with one row per group and three columns named 'mean', 'std', and 'count'

MC: Why does Chapter 10 recommend passing string aliases like 'mean' or 'sum' to .agg() rather than the equivalent NumPy functions like np.mean or np.sum?

column3: String aliases are the only way to apply a function to a GroupBy object; NumPy functions will raise an error
NumPy functions do not handle missing values, while string aliases do
String aliases automatically round results to two decimal places
The string aliases use optimized implementations that are faster than general custom functions

MC: When you pass a list of (name, function) tuples to .agg() instead of a plain list of function names, what does the tuple form control?

column3: The first element of each tuple becomes the column name in the resulting DataFrame, replacing the default function name
The first element restricts which groups the function runs on; the second is the function itself
The tuple form changes the order of operations so the named step runs before any plain-list steps
The tuple form is only used to pass keyword arguments to the aggregation function

MC: If you pass a dictionary like {'total_bill': 'sum', 'tip': 'mean'} to .agg(), what happens?

column3: The keys become the column names in the output and the values become the row labels
Each column listed in the dictionary is aggregated using its own specified function; columns not listed are excluded from the result
All listed functions are applied to all listed columns, producing a DataFrame with hierarchical columns
The first key-value pair is used and the others are silently ignored

MC: When aggregating a DataFrame with multiple value columns and passing a list of functions to .agg(), what shape does the resulting DataFrame have?

column3: A flat column index with names like 'column1_mean', 'column1_sum', produced by joining the column name and function name
A DataFrame with the original columns and a new index level labeling which function produced each row
A Series with a MultiIndex combining column name and function name
A DataFrame with hierarchical columns: an outer level naming the source column and an inner level naming the function

MC: What does passing as_index=False to groupby() change about the aggregation result?

column3: The result retains every original row rather than reducing to one row per group
The grouping keys become regular columns in the result rather than being placed in the index
Missing values in the grouping key become their own group in the result
The result is returned as a NumPy array instead of a DataFrame

MC: Which GroupBy method is described in Chapter 10 as the 'most general-purpose'?

column3: agg
transform
apply
filter

MC: When you pass a function to grouped.apply(f), what does pandas do with it?

column3: It splits the data into groups, calls f on each group, and concatenates the results into a single output
It runs f once on the full DataFrame and then filters the result by group
It converts f into a vectorized NumPy operation and applies it element-wise within each group
It raises an error unless f returns a single scalar value; apply requires reduction

MC: How do you pass extra keyword arguments to a function used with grouped.apply()?

column3: Wrap the function in a lambda that captures the arguments, for example grouped.apply(lambda g: f(g, n=5)), which is the only supported pattern
Pass them after the function itself in the apply call, for example grouped.apply(f, n=5, column='col_name')
Set them as attributes on the GroupBy object before calling apply
Keyword arguments are not supported with apply; the function must be rewritten to take only one argument

MC: What is the key difference between the output of grouped.apply() and grouped.agg() when the same function is used?

column3: apply always returns a Python list; agg returns a DataFrame
apply only works on a single column at a time; agg works on the full DataFrame
apply can return any shape per group; agg expects one value per group
apply drops rows with NaN values; agg retains them

MC: What does passing group_keys=False to groupby() do when combined with apply()?

column3: It suppresses the hierarchical index formed from the group keys in the apply result, leaving only the original row indexes
It causes apply to drop the grouping columns from the result
It changes apply to return a dictionary keyed by group name instead of a DataFrame
It skips groups whose key is NaN

MC: When you call grouped.describe(), what is Chapter 10's explanation of how pandas computes the result internally?

column3: It is equivalent to calling .describe() on the full DataFrame and then grouping the output
It uses a dedicated SQL-style implementation that bypasses the GroupBy mechanism
It is an alias for grouped.agg(['mean', 'std', 'min', 'max'])
It is equivalent to writing a function that calls .describe() on a group and passing that function to grouped.apply()

MC: To compute group statistics on quantile-defined buckets of a numeric column, what pattern does Chapter 10 describe?

column3: Use pd.qcut() or pd.cut() to produce a Categorical, then pass that Categorical directly to groupby() as the grouping key
Call .quantile() on the GroupBy object with a list of quantile cutoffs
Call pd.qcut() on the result of a GroupBy aggregation, passing the number of desired buckets
Manually sort the DataFrame, slice it into N equal-sized chunks, and aggregate each chunk separately

MC: Which of the following is NOT one of the three constraints Chapter 10 lists for a function passed to transform()?

column3: It can produce a scalar value that will be broadcast to the shape of the group
It can produce an object of the same shape as the input group
It must reduce the input to a single value per group, like an aggregation
It must not mutate its input

MC: If grouped.mean() produces a 3-row Series (one row per group), what shape will grouped.transform('mean') produce on the same GroupBy?

column3: A 3-row Series identical to grouped.mean()
A 1-row Series containing the overall mean across all groups
A DataFrame with three rows and one column per original column
The same number of rows as the original input, with the group mean broadcast to every row that belongs to the group

MC: What does Chapter 10 mean by an 'unwrapped' group operation?

column3: Calling .reset_index() on a grouped result to flatten the hierarchical index
Using groupby on an already-sorted DataFrame so no splitting is needed
Performing arithmetic directly between GroupBy transform outputs and original Series, rather than wrapping the whole calculation in a function passed to apply
Applying a transform function to the full DataFrame instead of to individual groups

MC: In a pivot_table() call, what does the values argument specify?

column3: The column (or list of columns) whose values are aggregated in the cells of the result
The list of allowed values that the index and columns can take
The fill value to use where a cell combination has no matching rows
The set of unique values to use as column labels in the output

MC: What is the default aggregation function used by pivot_table() when the aggfunc argument is not specified?

column3: sum
count
mean
first

MC: What does the margins=True argument add to a pivot_table() result?

column3: A visual border drawn around the outer edge of the printed table
An 'All' row and 'All' column with totals across each axis, plus a grand total in the bottom-right
A 'Margin' column at the right of the table showing the standard error for each row
Row and column labels showing the percentage each cell contributes to its row total
A specialized pivot_table for frequency counts, taking arrays or Series as its first two arguments
A shortcut for pivot_table with aggfunc='sum' and a DataFrame as its first argument
A function that joins two DataFrames by combining every row of one with every row of the other
A helper for reshaping between wide and long format, similar to pd.melt
